title: Difference Between SS 304 and SS 316 Pipe — Complete Guide
description: Learn the key differences between SS 304 and SS 316 stainless steel pipes including chemical composition, corrosion resistance, applications, and pricing to make the right choice.
keywords: SS 304 vs SS 316, stainless steel 304 vs 316, difference between 304 and 316 pipe, SS 304 pipe, SS 316 pipe, stainless steel pipe comparison, 304 vs 316 corrosion resistance
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Stainless steel 304 and 316 are the two most widely used austenitic stainless steel grades in the piping industry. While they may look identical, their chemical composition, mechanical properties, and corrosion resistance differ significantly — and choosing the wrong grade can lead to premature failure, safety risks, and unnecessary costs.

This guide breaks down every key difference between SS 304 and SS 316 pipes to help you make an informed material selection for your project.

sections.heading: Chemical Composition Comparison
sections.content:
The primary difference between 304 and 316 lies in their chemical composition:

**SS 304 (UNS S30400):**
• Chromium: 18–20%
• Nickel: 8–10.5%
• Carbon: 0.08% max
• Manganese: 2% max

**SS 316 (UNS S31600):**
• Chromium: 16–18%
• Nickel: 10–14%
• Molybdenum: 2–3% ← Key difference
• Carbon: 0.08% max

The critical difference is **molybdenum** — SS 316 contains 2-3% molybdenum which dramatically improves resistance to chloride and acid corrosion. This single element addition makes 316 the preferred choice for marine, chemical, and pharmaceutical applications.

sections.heading: Corrosion Resistance
sections.content:
**SS 304:** Excellent general corrosion resistance. Performs well in most atmospheric and freshwater environments. However, it is susceptible to pitting and crevice corrosion in chloride-rich environments (seawater, coastal areas, de-icing salts).

**SS 316:** Superior corrosion resistance due to molybdenum content. Resists pitting corrosion in chloride environments, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, and bromide solutions. The go-to grade for marine, chemical processing, and pharmaceutical industries.

**Key takeaway:** If your application involves exposure to saltwater, chemicals, or acids, SS 316 is the clear choice. For general indoor/atmospheric applications, SS 304 offers excellent performance at lower cost.

sections.heading: Mechanical Properties
sections.content:
Both grades share similar mechanical properties:

| Property | SS 304 | SS 316 |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 515 MPa min | 515 MPa min |
| Yield Strength | 205 MPa min | 205 MPa min |
| Elongation | 40% min | 40% min |
| Hardness (Brinell) | 201 max | 217 max |
| Density | 8.0 g/cm³ | 8.0 g/cm³ |
| Melting Point | 1400–1450°C | 1375–1400°C |

Both grades are non-magnetic in annealed condition, highly weldable, and can be cold-worked to increase strength.

sections.heading: Applications
sections.content:
**SS 304 is commonly used in:**
• Kitchen equipment and food processing
• Architectural trim and panels
• Chemical containers and tanks
• Heat exchangers (non-chloride service)
• Automotive parts
• Brewery and dairy equipment

**SS 316 is preferred for:**
• Marine and offshore equipment
• Chemical and petrochemical processing
• Pharmaceutical and biotech equipment
• Surgical instruments and medical devices
• Coastal architectural applications
• Pulp and paper mill equipment
• Desalination plants

sections.heading: Price Comparison
sections.content:
SS 316 pipes typically cost **20-30% more** than SS 304 pipes due to the higher nickel content and addition of molybdenum. However, in corrosive environments, the longer service life of 316 often results in lower lifecycle costs.

**Cost-saving tip:** If corrosion resistance beyond 304 is needed but budget is a concern, consider SS 316L (low carbon variant) which offers the same corrosion resistance as 316 with improved weldability, and sometimes at competitive pricing.

sections.heading: Which Grade Should You Choose?
sections.content:
**Choose SS 304 when:**
• The environment is non-corrosive or mildly corrosive
• Cost is a primary concern
• The application is indoor or atmospheric
• Food-grade requirements without chloride exposure

**Choose SS 316 when:**
• Chloride or saltwater exposure is expected
• Chemical or acid resistance is required
• Marine, offshore, or coastal applications
• Pharmaceutical or medical-grade requirements
• Long-term reliability in harsh environments is critical

Starlight Tubes manufactures both SS 304 and SS 316/316L pipes in seamless and welded configurations per ASTM A312/A213 standards. Contact our team for grade-specific pricing and availability.

faqSchema.question: What is the main difference between SS 304 and SS 316?
faqSchema.answer: The main difference is that SS 316 contains 2-3% molybdenum, which gives it superior corrosion resistance against chlorides and acids compared to SS 304. This makes 316 ideal for marine, chemical, and pharmaceutical applications.

faqSchema.question: Is SS 316 stronger than SS 304?
faqSchema.answer: Both grades have similar tensile strength (515 MPa min) and yield strength (205 MPa min). The advantage of 316 is not in mechanical strength but in corrosion resistance, particularly against chlorides, sulfuric acid, and pitting corrosion.

faqSchema.question: How much more expensive is SS 316 compared to SS 304?
faqSchema.answer: SS 316 pipes typically cost 20-30% more than SS 304 due to higher nickel content and the addition of molybdenum. However, in corrosive environments, 316 often provides lower lifecycle costs due to its longer service life.

title: API 5L Grade B vs ASTM A106 — Complete Comparison Guide
description: Comprehensive comparison of API 5L Grade B and ASTM A106 carbon steel pipes covering specifications, chemical composition, applications, testing requirements, and how to choose the right standard.
keywords: API 5L vs ASTM A106, API 5L Grade B pipe, ASTM A106 Grade B pipe, carbon steel pipe comparison, line pipe vs seamless pipe, API 5L PSL1 PSL2, A106 Grade B specifications
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
API 5L and ASTM A106 are the two most commonly specified carbon steel pipe standards in the oil & gas and petrochemical industries. While they overlap in some applications, they serve fundamentally different purposes — and specifying the wrong standard can lead to project delays, compliance issues, or safety failures.

This guide provides a detailed comparison to help engineers, procurement teams, and project managers choose the right specification.

sections.heading: Standard Overview
sections.content:
**API 5L (American Petroleum Institute):**
Covers welded and seamless line pipes for the transportation of gas, water, and oil in the petroleum and natural gas industries. It includes two Product Specification Levels: PSL1 (standard quality) and PSL2 (enhanced quality with stricter chemistry, testing, and traceability requirements).

**ASTM A106 (American Society for Testing and Materials):**
Covers seamless carbon steel pipe for high-temperature service in refineries, power plants, and petrochemical plants. Available in three grades: Grade A (least strong), Grade B (most commonly used), and Grade C (highest strength).

sections.heading: Chemical Composition
sections.content:
**API 5L Grade B:**
• Carbon: 0.28% max (PSL1) / 0.24% max (PSL2)
• Manganese: 1.20% max (PSL1) / 1.20% max (PSL2)
• Phosphorus: 0.030% max
• Sulfur: 0.030% max
• Carbon Equivalent (CE): ≤ 0.43 (PSL2)

**ASTM A106 Grade B:**
• Carbon: 0.30% max
• Manganese: 0.29–1.06%
• Phosphorus: 0.035% max
• Sulfur: 0.035% max
• Silicon: 0.10% min

Notably, API 5L PSL2 has stricter chemistry controls including a mandatory Carbon Equivalent limit, making it more suitable for welding in critical applications.

sections.heading: Mechanical Properties
sections.content:
| Property | API 5L Gr. B | ASTM A106 Gr. B |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength (min) | 245 MPa (35.5 ksi) | 240 MPa (35 ksi) |
| Tensile Strength (min) | 415 MPa (60 ksi) | 415 MPa (60 ksi) |
| Elongation | Varies by WT | 30% min |

The mechanical properties are very similar, which is why these two specifications are often considered interchangeable for certain applications — though they are NOT the same standard.

sections.heading: Key Differences
sections.content:
**1. Manufacturing Method:**
• API 5L: Both seamless AND welded (ERW, SAW, DSAW) pipes
• ASTM A106: Seamless ONLY

**2. Primary Application:**
• API 5L: Oil & gas pipeline transportation (line pipe)
• ASTM A106: High-temperature process piping in refineries/power plants

**3. Testing Requirements:**
• API 5L PSL2: Mandatory CVN impact testing, stricter NDE, full traceability
• ASTM A106: Hydrostatic test only (supplementary tests on request)

**4. Welded Options:**
• API 5L: Available as ERW, DSAW, seamless
• ASTM A106: Seamless only

**5. Temperature Service:**
• API 5L: Ambient and moderate temperature pipeline service
• ASTM A106: Specifically designed for elevated temperature service (up to ~425°C)

sections.heading: When to Use Which Standard
sections.content:
**Use API 5L Grade B when:**
• Building oil, gas, or water transmission pipelines
• Onshore or offshore pipeline applications
• You need welded pipe options (ERW/DSAW)
• Pipeline codes (ASME B31.4 / B31.8) govern the project
• PSL2 is needed for sour service (H₂S environments)

**Use ASTM A106 Grade B when:**
• High-temperature process piping in refineries/power plants
• Boiler tubes and superheater lines
• Steam piping systems
• Process piping governed by ASME B31.1 or B31.3
• Only seamless pipe is acceptable

Starlight Tubes manufactures both API 5L Grade B (PSL1 & PSL2) and ASTM A106 Grade B seamless pipes with full Mill Test Certificates (EN 10204 3.1).

faqSchema.question: Can API 5L Grade B and ASTM A106 Grade B be used interchangeably?
faqSchema.answer: In some applications they can be substituted because their chemistry and mechanical properties overlap. However, they are designed for different purposes — API 5L for pipeline transport, ASTM A106 for high-temperature process piping. Always verify with the project specification and applicable piping code before substituting.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between API 5L PSL1 and PSL2?
faqSchema.answer: PSL2 has stricter requirements than PSL1 including mandatory carbon equivalent limits, mandatory impact testing, stricter chemistry tolerances, enhanced NDE requirements, and full material traceability. PSL2 is required for sour service (H₂S) and critical pipeline applications.

title: Complete Guide to Pipe Fittings — Types, Uses & Specifications
description: Comprehensive guide covering all pipe fitting types including elbows, tees, reducers, caps, unions, couplings, and flanges with their applications, materials, and ASME specifications.
keywords: pipe fittings types, pipe fittings guide, elbow fitting, tee fitting, reducer fitting, coupling fitting, union fitting, ASME B16.9, butt weld fittings, socket weld fittings, threaded fittings
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Pipe fittings are essential components used to connect, redirect, control, and terminate piping systems across industrial, commercial, and residential applications. Understanding the different types of pipe fittings — their functions, connection methods, materials, and applicable standards — is critical for every piping engineer, plumber, and procurement professional.

This guide covers all major pipe fitting types with their specifications and applications.

sections.heading: Types of Pipe Fittings by Function
sections.content:
Pipe fittings can be categorized by their primary function:

**Direction Change:** Elbows (45°, 90°, 180°)
**Branching:** Tees (Equal, Reducing), Crosses, Wyes
**Size Change:** Reducers (Concentric, Eccentric), Swages
**Termination:** Caps, Plugs, Blinds
**Connection:** Couplings (Full, Half, Reducing), Unions, Nipples
**Flow Control:** Valves, Strainers
**Support:** Flanges, Stub Ends, Backing Rings

sections.heading: Elbows
sections.content:
Elbows change the direction of flow in a piping system and are classified by their angle:

**90° Elbow:** Most common, creates a right-angle turn. Available as long radius (LR, radius = 1.5D) and short radius (SR, radius = 1D).

**45° Elbow:** Creates a 45-degree change in direction. Used where a gentler turn is needed to reduce pressure drop.

**180° Return Bend:** Reverses the flow direction completely. Used in tube bundles and heat exchangers.

**Standards:** ASME B16.9 (butt weld), ASME B16.11 (socket weld & threaded)
**Materials:** SS 304/316, Carbon Steel A234 WPB, Alloy Steel, Nickel Alloys

sections.heading: Tees and Reducers
sections.content:
**Tees** create branch connections from a main pipe run:
• **Equal Tee:** All three openings are the same size
• **Reducing Tee:** Branch opening is smaller than the run openings
• **Barred Tee:** Has internal bars to prevent pig passage into the branch

**Reducers** connect pipes of different diameters:
• **Concentric Reducer:** Centers axis remains the same — used in vertical lines
• **Eccentric Reducer:** One side is flat — used in horizontal lines to prevent air pockets (flat-on-bottom) or liquid accumulation (flat-on-top)

sections.heading: Connection Methods
sections.content:
**Butt Weld Fittings (ASME B16.9):**
• Joined by welding pipe ends together
• Used for sizes 1/2" and larger
• Strongest permanent joint
• Requires qualified welders and NDE inspection

**Socket Weld Fittings (ASME B16.11):**
• Pipe inserts into a recessed area (socket)
• Fillet weld on the outside
• Used for small-bore piping (≤ 2" NPS)
• Class ratings: 3000#, 6000#, 9000#

**Threaded Fittings (ASME B16.11):**
• NPT, BSP, or BSPT threaded connections
• No welding required (can be assembled/disassembled)
• Used for small-bore, low-pressure applications
• Class ratings: 2000#, 3000#, 6000#

**Flanged Connections (ASME B16.5):**
• Bolted connection using gaskets
• Allows easy disassembly for maintenance
• Pressure classes: 150#, 300#, 600#, 900#, 1500#, 2500#

sections.heading: Material Selection
sections.content:
The pipe fitting material must match the pipe material and service conditions:

**Stainless Steel (A403 WP304/316):** Chemical plants, food processing, pharmaceutical
**Carbon Steel (A234 WPB):** General industrial, oil & gas, power plants
**Low Temp Carbon Steel (A420 WPL6):** Cryogenic and low-temperature applications
**Alloy Steel (A234 WP5/WP9/WP11/WP22):** High temperature and pressure service
**Nickel Alloys (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy):** Extreme corrosion and temperature resistance
**Duplex/Super Duplex:** Offshore, subsea, and high-chloride environments

Starlight Tubes manufactures pipe fittings in all these materials per ASME, ASTM, and MSS standards.

faqSchema.question: What are the main types of pipe fittings?
faqSchema.answer: The main types are: Elbows (45°, 90°, 180°) for direction change, Tees for branching, Reducers (concentric, eccentric) for size change, Caps and Plugs for termination, Couplings and Unions for connection, and Flanges for bolted connections. They're available in butt weld, socket weld, and threaded connection types.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between butt weld and socket weld fittings?
faqSchema.answer: Butt weld fittings (ASME B16.9) are joined by welding pipe ends together and are used for larger sizes (1/2" and above). Socket weld fittings (ASME B16.11) have a recessed socket where the pipe inserts, then a fillet weld is applied — used for small-bore piping up to 2" NPS in high-pressure applications.

title: Inconel 625 vs Inconel 600 — Properties, Differences & Applications
description: Detailed comparison of Inconel 625 and Inconel 600 superalloys covering chemical composition, mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, and applications to help you choose the right alloy.
keywords: Inconel 625 vs 600, Inconel 625 properties, Inconel 600 properties, UNS N06625 vs N06600, nickel alloy comparison, Inconel pipe, superalloy pipe, high temperature alloy
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Inconel 625 (UNS N06625) and Inconel 600 (UNS N06600) are both nickel-chromium superalloys renowned for their exceptional performance in extreme environments. However, they have distinct compositions, properties, and ideal applications that make each uniquely suited to specific service conditions.

This guide provides a comprehensive comparison to help engineers and material specifiers choose between these two premium alloys.

sections.heading: Chemical Composition
sections.content:
**Inconel 625 (UNS N06625):**
• Nickel: 58% min (balance)
• Chromium: 20–23%
• Molybdenum: 8–10% ← Key differentiator
• Niobium (Columbium): 3.15–4.15% ← Key differentiator
• Iron: 5% max
• Carbon: 0.10% max

**Inconel 600 (UNS N06600):**
• Nickel: 72% min (balance)
• Chromium: 14–17%
• Iron: 6–10%
• Carbon: 0.15% max
• Manganese: 1% max

The key differences: Inconel 625 contains significant amounts of **molybdenum** (8-10%) and **niobium** (3.15-4.15%) which are absent in Inconel 600. These additions give 625 its superior corrosion resistance and higher strength. Meanwhile, Inconel 600 has a higher nickel content (72% vs 58%).

sections.heading: Mechanical Properties
sections.content:
| Property | Inconel 625 | Inconel 600 |
|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength | 827 MPa (120 ksi) min | 550 MPa (80 ksi) min |
| Yield Strength | 414 MPa (60 ksi) min | 240 MPa (35 ksi) min |
| Elongation | 30% min | 30% min |
| Hardness | 175-240 HB | 120-170 HB |
| Density | 8.44 g/cm³ | 8.47 g/cm³ |
| Melting Range | 1290–1350°C | 1354–1413°C |
| Max Service Temp | ~980°C | ~1095°C |

Inconel 625 is significantly stronger due to niobium-strengthened solid-solution hardening. Inconel 600, with its higher melting point, can operate at slightly higher temperatures.

sections.heading: Corrosion Resistance
sections.content:
**Inconel 625:** Exceptional resistance to a wide range of corrosive media including seawater, acids (phosphoric, sulfuric, hydrochloric), and chloride-induced stress corrosion cracking. The high molybdenum content provides outstanding pitting and crevice corrosion resistance. Resistant to chloride stress corrosion cracking — superior to most stainless steels and many nickel alloys.

**Inconel 600:** Good oxidation resistance at high temperatures. Resistant to many organic and inorganic compounds. Good performance in alkaline solutions and dry chlorine/hydrogen chloride. However, less resistant than 625 to pitting, crevice corrosion, and reducing acid environments.

**Conclusion:** For corrosion-critical applications (marine, chemical processing, subsea), Inconel 625 is the superior choice. For high-temperature oxidation resistance (furnaces, heat treating), Inconel 600 performs well.

sections.heading: Applications
sections.content:
**Inconel 625 Applications:**
• Marine and offshore engineering (subsea equipment, flexible risers)
• Chemical processing (reactors, heat exchangers, piping)
• Aerospace (jet engine exhaust systems, thrust reversers)
• Flue gas desulfurization systems
• Nuclear water reactors
• Oil & gas well completion equipment
• Pollution control equipment

**Inconel 600 Applications:**
• Heat treating equipment (furnace muffles, retorts, baskets)
• Nuclear engineering (nuclear reactor components)
• Chemical processing (organic and inorganic compounds)
• Aerospace (gas turbine components)
• Electronics (leads for transistors)
• Food processing equipment

sections.heading: Which Alloy Should You Choose?
sections.content:
**Choose Inconel 625 when:**
• Superior corrosion resistance is required (marine, subsea, chemical)
• High strength combined with corrosion resistance is needed
• Seawater or chloride exposure is expected
• Acid resistance (sulfuric, hydrochloric, phosphoric) is critical
• The application involves welding in corrosive environments

**Choose Inconel 600 when:**
• High-temperature resistance above 980°C is needed
• Furnace and heat-treating applications
• Nuclear reactor components
• Cost is a concern (600 is typically less expensive than 625)
• Dry gas or oxidizing environments at elevated temperatures

Starlight Tubes manufactures both Inconel 625 (ASTM B444/B705) and Inconel 600 (ASTM B167/B516) pipes and tubes. Contact us for specifications and pricing.

faqSchema.question: What is the main difference between Inconel 625 and Inconel 600?
faqSchema.answer: Inconel 625 contains 8-10% molybdenum and 3.15-4.15% niobium, which give it superior corrosion resistance and higher strength compared to Inconel 600. Inconel 600 has higher nickel content (72% vs 58%) and can operate at higher temperatures (up to 1095°C vs 980°C for 625).

faqSchema.question: Which is more expensive, Inconel 625 or Inconel 600?
faqSchema.answer: Inconel 625 is generally more expensive than Inconel 600 due to its higher molybdenum and niobium content. However, in corrosive service environments, 625's superior corrosion resistance can result in lower lifecycle costs due to extended service life.

title: What is Cupro-Nickel 90/10? Properties, Uses & Specifications
description: Complete guide to Cupro-Nickel 90/10 (C70600) covering composition, mechanical properties, corrosion resistance, marine applications, and ASTM specifications for pipes and tubes.
keywords: cupro nickel 90/10, C70600 pipe, copper nickel 90 10, CuNi 90/10, cupro nickel pipe, ASTM B466, ASTM B111, marine pipe, seawater corrosion resistant pipe
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Cupro-Nickel 90/10 (also known as CuNi 90/10 or by its UNS designation C70600) is a copper-nickel alloy containing approximately 90% copper and 10% nickel. It is one of the most widely used corrosion-resistant alloys for seawater and marine applications.

This guide covers everything you need to know about CuNi 90/10 — from its composition and properties to its applications and the standards it must meet.

sections.heading: Chemical Composition
sections.content:
**Cupro-Nickel 90/10 (UNS C70600):**
• Copper: Balance (~88%)
• Nickel: 10–11%
• Iron: 1.0–1.8%
• Manganese: 1.0% max
• Zinc: 0.5% max
• Lead: 0.02% max
• Carbon: 0.05% max
• Sulfur: 0.02% max

The addition of iron (1-1.8%) is crucial — it dramatically improves the alloy's resistance to impingement attack and erosion-corrosion in flowing seawater, which is why iron content is carefully controlled.

sections.heading: Properties
sections.content:
**Mechanical Properties:**
• Tensile Strength: 275 MPa min (annealed)
• Yield Strength: 105 MPa min
• Elongation: 30% min
• Hardness: 100-140 HB
• Density: 8.94 g/cm³

**Key Characteristics:**
• Excellent resistance to seawater corrosion and biofouling
• Good resistance to stress corrosion cracking
• Inherent antimicrobial properties (copper ions inhibit marine organism attachment)
• Good thermal conductivity for heat exchanger applications
• Low macrofouling rate — marine organisms don't easily attach
• Good weldability with both TIG and MIG processes
• Can be readily fabricated using standard copper working techniques

sections.heading: Marine and Industrial Applications
sections.content:
CuNi 90/10 is the material of choice for seawater systems:

**Marine Applications:**
• Seawater piping systems on ships and offshore platforms
• Desalination plant condenser and heat exchanger tubes
• Seawater cooling systems
• Offshore platform firewater systems
• Ship hulls and propellers (biofouling resistance)
• LNG plant seawater intakes

**Industrial Applications:**
• Power plant condenser tubes
• Chemical process heat exchangers
• Hydraulic brake lines
• Fresh water piping in coastal installations

**Why not just use stainless steel?** While SS 316 has good general corrosion resistance, CuNi 90/10 has better biofouling resistance, is more resistant to crevice corrosion in low-velocity seawater, and doesn't suffer the same microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC) issues as stainless steel.

sections.heading: Applicable Standards
sections.content:
CuNi 90/10 products are manufactured per these standards:

**Pipes:**
• ASTM B466 / ASME SB466 — Seamless Copper-Nickel Pipe
• ASTM B467 — Welded Copper-Nickel Pipe

**Tubes:**
• ASTM B111 / ASME SB111 — Copper-Nickel Heat Exchanger & Condenser Tubes
• ASTM B552 — Copper-Nickel Tube (general service)

**Fittings:**
• ASME B16.22 — Wrought Copper and Copper Alloy Fittings
• MSS SP-104 — Wrought Copper Solder Joint Pressure Fittings

**Flanges:**
• ASME B16.24 — Copper Alloy Pipe Flanges

Starlight Tubes supplies CuNi 90/10 pipes per ASTM B466, tubes per ASTM B111, and fittings per ASME B16.22 with full Mill Test Certificates.

sections.heading: CuNi 90/10 vs 70/30 — When to Choose Which
sections.content:
**CuNi 90/10 (C70600):**
• Lower cost than 70/30
• Adequate for most seawater systems
• Standard choice for seawater piping up to 3.5 m/s flow velocity
• Typical for ship seawater systems, coastal power plants

**CuNi 70/30 (C71500):**
• Higher nickel content → better corrosion resistance
• Handles higher flow velocities (up to 4.5 m/s)
• Better for highly polluted or high-temperature seawater
• Used in condenser tubes for coastal/nuclear power plants
• Higher cost (roughly 30-50% more than 90/10)

**Rule of thumb:** Use 90/10 as your standard choice; upgrade to 70/30 only if the seawater conditions are particularly aggressive or flow velocities exceed 3.5 m/s.

faqSchema.question: What is Cupro-Nickel 90/10 used for?
faqSchema.answer: CuNi 90/10 (C70600) is primarily used in seawater and marine applications including ship seawater piping systems, desalination plant condenser tubes, offshore firewater systems, heat exchangers, and power plant cooling systems. Its excellent resistance to seawater corrosion and biofouling makes it the preferred material for these applications.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between CuNi 90/10 and 70/30?
faqSchema.answer: CuNi 90/10 (C70600) contains 10% nickel while 70/30 (C71500) contains 30% nickel. The higher nickel content in 70/30 provides better corrosion resistance and can handle higher seawater flow velocities (up to 4.5 m/s vs 3.5 m/s). However, 70/30 costs 30-50% more. Use 90/10 as the standard choice and upgrade to 70/30 for aggressive conditions.

title: Understanding Carbon Steel Pipe Grades — A53, A106, A671, A672
description: Complete guide to carbon steel pipe grades including ASTM A53, ASTM A106, ASTM A671, and ASTM A672 covering specifications, manufacturing methods, grades, and applications.
keywords: carbon steel pipe grades, ASTM A53, ASTM A106, ASTM A671, ASTM A672, carbon steel pipe specifications, seamless pipe grades, ERW pipe grades, EFW pipe grades, carbon steel pipe types
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Carbon steel pipes are the backbone of industrial piping systems worldwide. However, not all carbon steel pipes are the same — different ASTM standards cover different manufacturing methods, service conditions, and quality levels.

The four most important carbon steel pipe standards are ASTM A53, ASTM A106, ASTM A671, and ASTM A672. Understanding the differences between these grades is essential for proper material specification and safe piping system design.

sections.heading: ASTM A53 — The General Purpose Standard
sections.content:
**ASTM A53** covers welded and seamless black and hot-dipped galvanized steel pipes for general applications.

**Manufacturing Types:**
• Type F: Furnace-butt welded, continuous welded
• Type E: Electric-Resistance Welded (ERW)
• Type S: Seamless

**Grades:**
• Grade A: Minimum tensile 330 MPa, yield 205 MPa
• Grade B: Minimum tensile 415 MPa, yield 240 MPa

**Applications:** General structural, water wells, air lines, steam lines (low pressure), fencing, scaffolding, general mechanical & pressure applications.

**Key Feature:** Available in both black and galvanized finish. Grade B is the most commonly specified.

sections.heading: ASTM A106 — The High-Temperature Standard
sections.content:
**ASTM A106** covers seamless carbon steel pipe for high-temperature service.

**Manufacturing:** Seamless ONLY (no welded option)

**Grades:**
• Grade A: Tensile 330 MPa min, Yield 205 MPa min
• Grade B: Tensile 415 MPa min, Yield 240 MPa min (most common)
• Grade C: Tensile 485 MPa min, Yield 275 MPa min (highest strength)

**Applications:** High-temperature boiler tubes, process piping in refineries, steam lines, heat exchangers, superheater tubes, and any application where temperatures exceed ordinary levels.

**Key Feature:** Specifically designed and tested for elevated temperature service. Every pipe is hydrostatic tested. Most widely used specification for process piping in oil & gas and petrochemical industries.

sections.heading: ASTM A671 — EFW for Atmospheric Temperature
sections.content:
**ASTM A671** covers electric fusion welded (EFW) steel pipes for atmospheric and lower temperature service.

**Manufacturing:** Electric Fusion Welded (double submerged arc welded / DSAW)

**Classification System:**
• First letter(s): Radiographic examination class (C = 100% radiography, E = spot radiography)
• Number: Plate specification (e.g., 60 = A516 Gr 60, 65 = A516 Gr 65)
• Examples: CC60, CC65, CC70, CE55, CE60

**Applications:** Power plants, refineries, low-temperature pressure piping, atmospheric service, and large-diameter pipeline applications.

**Key Feature:** Made from pressure vessel quality steel plates (A516 series) rolled and welded. Available in large diameters (16" to 72") that are not economically feasible as seamless.

sections.heading: ASTM A672 — EFW for High-Pressure Service
sections.content:
**ASTM A672** covers electric fusion welded steel pipe for high-pressure service at moderate temperatures.

**Manufacturing:** Electric Fusion Welded (DSAW)

**Classification System:** Same as A671 (CC60, CC65, CC70, etc.)

**Key Difference from A671:** A672 has MORE STRINGENT requirements:
• Additional pressure testing requirements
• Stricter NDE (Non-Destructive Examination) requirements
• Higher design factors for pressure calculations
• Mandatory supplementary examination

**Applications:** High-pressure steam lines, high-pressure process piping, main steam piping in power plants, critical pressure service applications.

**Key Feature:** When A671 isn't enough — use A672 for higher-pressure and more critical applications using the same EFW manufacturing process.

sections.heading: Comparison Summary
sections.content:
| Feature | A53 | A106 | A671 | A672 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Seamless + Welded | Seamless Only | EFW | EFW |
| Temperature | Normal | High | Low/Atmospheric | Moderate |
| Pressure | Low/Medium | High | Medium | High |
| Sizes | 1/8" - 26" | 1/4" - 26" | 16" - 72" | 16" - 72" |
| Galvanized | Available | No | No | No |
| Primary Use | General | Process Piping | Power Plants | High-P Steam |

Starlight Tubes manufactures pipes per all four standards. Contact us for detailed specifications, sizes, and pricing.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between ASTM A53 and ASTM A106 pipe?
faqSchema.answer: ASTM A53 covers both welded and seamless pipes for general applications and is available in galvanized finish. ASTM A106 covers seamless pipes only, specifically designed for high-temperature service in refineries and power plants. A106 has stricter chemistry and testing requirements for elevated temperature performance.

faqSchema.question: What does CC65 mean in ASTM A671/A672?
faqSchema.answer: In ASTM A671/A672, the 'C' prefix indicates 100% radiographic examination of weld seams, and '65' refers to the plate material (A516 Grade 65). So CC65 means an EFW pipe made from A516 Gr.65 plate with 100% radiographic inspection of all welds.

title: How to Read a Mill Test Certificate (MTC) — Complete Guide
description: Step-by-step guide on how to read and verify a Mill Test Certificate (MTC / MTR) per EN 10204 3.1 including chemical composition, mechanical properties, heat numbers, and acceptance criteria.
keywords: mill test certificate, MTC, MTR, mill test report, EN 10204 3.1, how to read MTC, chemical composition certificate, mechanical properties certificate, heat number, steel certificate
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
A Mill Test Certificate (MTC) — also called a Mill Test Report (MTR) or Material Test Certificate — is a quality assurance document provided by the steel manufacturer that certifies the chemical and mechanical properties of a steel product. It is a critical document for traceability, quality verification, and regulatory compliance in the piping industry.

Every engineer, QA/QC inspector, and procurement professional should know how to read and verify an MTC. This guide shows you exactly how.

sections.heading: What is EN 10204 3.1?
sections.content:
EN 10204 defines five types of inspection documents:

**Type 2.1:** Declaration of compliance (manufacturer states compliance, no test data)
**Type 2.2:** Test report (non-specific test results from similar products)
**Type 3.1:** Inspection certificate 3.1 (specific test results from the actual ordered product, issued by the manufacturer)
**Type 3.2:** Inspection certificate 3.2 (same as 3.1 but validated by an independent inspector)

**EN 10204 3.1 is the most commonly required** document type in the piping industry. It contains actual test results from the specific heat of material that was used to manufacture your product.

sections.heading: Key Sections of an MTC
sections.content:
A standard MTC contains these sections:

**1. Header Information:**
• Manufacturer name and address
• Certificate number and date
• Customer name and purchase order reference

**2. Product Description:**
• Material specification (e.g., ASTM A312 TP316L)
• Product type (seamless pipe, welded tube, etc.)
• Dimensions (OD, wall thickness, length)
• Quantity and weight
• Heat number / lot number

**3. Chemical Composition:**
• Percentage of each element (C, Mn, Si, P, S, Cr, Ni, Mo, etc.)
• Listed alongside the specification limits for comparison

**4. Mechanical Properties:**
• Tensile strength (in MPa or ksi)
• Yield strength (0.2% proof stress)
• Elongation (%)
• Hardness (HB, HRC, or HV)

**5. Test Results:**
• Hydrostatic test pressure and result
• Non-destructive examination results (UT, RT, ET)
• Intergranular corrosion test (if applicable)
• Flattening, flaring, or bend test results

**6. Certification:**
• Authorized signatory name and stamp
• Statement of compliance
• Third-party inspector stamp (for 3.2 certificates)

sections.heading: How to Verify an MTC
sections.content:
Follow this checklist when reviewing an MTC:

**Step 1 — Verify the Specification:**
Confirm the material specification matches your purchase order (e.g., ASTM A312 TP316L).

**Step 2 — Check Chemical Composition:**
Compare every element against the specification limits. All values must be within the specified range. Pay special attention to:
• Carbon (C) — affects weldability
• Sulfur (S) & Phosphorus (P) — impurities, should be low
• Chromium (Cr) & Nickel (Ni) — must meet minimum for corrosion resistance

**Step 3 — Verify Mechanical Properties:**
• Tensile strength ≥ minimum specified
• Yield strength ≥ minimum specified
• Elongation ≥ minimum specified
• Hardness ≤ maximum specified

**Step 4 — Check Dimensions:**
Verify OD, wall thickness, and length match your order. Check tolerances per applicable standard.

**Step 5 — Confirm Testing:**
Ensure all required tests were performed and passed (hydro test, NDE, IGC if required).

**Step 6 — Verify Heat Number:**
The heat number on the MTC should match the heat number marked on the physical product.

**Step 7 — Check Signatures:**
The certificate must be signed by an authorized signatory. For 3.2 certificates, verify the third-party inspector's stamp.

sections.heading: Common Red Flags
sections.content:
Watch out for these issues when reviewing MTCs:

**⚠️ Values exactly at specification limits** — While technically acceptable, values sitting exactly on the limit (e.g., Carbon = 0.030% when max is 0.030%) warrant closer scrutiny.

**⚠️ Identical values across multiple certificates** — If different heat numbers show identical chemical compositions down to the last decimal, this could indicate fabricated data.

**⚠️ Missing test results** — If a required test (e.g., IGC test for 316L) is not mentioned at all, request clarification.

**⚠️ No heat number** — Every legitimate MTC must have a traceable heat number.

**⚠️ Generic signatures** — Legitimate certificates have specific authorized signatories, not generic stamps.

Starlight Tubes provides EN 10204 Type 3.1 Mill Test Certificates with every shipment. Third-party inspection (3.2) available on request.

faqSchema.question: What is a Mill Test Certificate (MTC)?
faqSchema.answer: A Mill Test Certificate (MTC), also called Mill Test Report (MTR), is a quality document provided by the steel manufacturer certifying the chemical composition and mechanical properties of a steel product. It is issued per EN 10204 standard and includes actual test results from the specific heat of material used to manufacture the product.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between EN 10204 Type 3.1 and 3.2?
faqSchema.answer: EN 10204 Type 3.1 certificates contain test results from the actual product, issued and signed by the manufacturer. Type 3.2 certificates have the same content but are additionally validated and countersigned by an independent third-party inspection body, providing an extra layer of verification.

title: Aluminium Alloy Grades — 5052, 6061, 6063, 7075 Comparison
description: Comprehensive comparison of aluminium alloy grades 5052, 6061, 6063, and 7075 covering composition, properties, applications, machinability, and weldability to help you select the right alloy.
keywords: aluminium alloy grades, aluminium 5052, aluminium 6061, aluminium 6063, aluminium 7075, aluminium grade comparison, aluminium pipe grades, aerospace aluminium, marine aluminium
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Aluminium alloys are organized by a four-digit numbering system where the first digit indicates the primary alloying element. The four most commonly used wrought aluminium alloys in industry — 5052, 6061, 6063, and 7075 — each have distinct properties that make them ideal for specific applications.

This guide provides a side-by-side comparison to help you select the right aluminium grade for your project.

sections.heading: Series Classification
sections.content:
**5xxx Series (Aluminium-Magnesium):**
• Primary alloying element: Magnesium
• Best corrosion resistance (especially marine)
• Not heat-treatable — strengthened by work hardening
• Example: 5052

**6xxx Series (Aluminium-Magnesium-Silicon):**
• Primary alloying elements: Magnesium + Silicon
• Good balance of strength, formability, and corrosion resistance
• Heat-treatable (T4, T6 tempers)
• Examples: 6061, 6063

**7xxx Series (Aluminium-Zinc):**
• Primary alloying element: Zinc
• Highest strength of all aluminium alloys
• Heat-treatable
• Moderate corrosion resistance
• Example: 7075

sections.heading: Properties Comparison
sections.content:
| Property | 5052-H32 | 6061-T6 | 6063-T6 | 7075-T6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 228 | 310 | 241 | 572 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 193 | 276 | 214 | 503 |
| Elongation (%) | 12 | 12 | 12 | 11 |
| Hardness (Brinell) | 60 | 95 | 73 | 150 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.68 | 2.70 | 2.69 | 2.81 |
| Machinability | Fair | Good | Fair | Good |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Poor |
| Corrosion Resistance | Excellent | Good | Good | Fair |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Low-Medium | High |

sections.heading: Applications
sections.content:
**Aluminium 5052:**
• Marine hulls and structures
• Fuel tanks and chemical vessels
• Road signs and traffic equipment
• Hydraulic tubes
• Home appliances
• Best choice for marine and saltwater exposure

**Aluminium 6061 (Most Versatile):**
• Aerospace structures
• Automotive frames and wheels
• Bicycle frames
• Bridge railings and walkways
• Screw machine products
• General structural applications

**Aluminium 6063 (Architectural Grade):**
• Window and door frames
• Architectural trim and panels
• Furniture and store fixtures
• Electrical conduit and pipe railings
• Irrigation pipes
• Best surface finish of all aluminium alloys

**Aluminium 7075 (Aerospace Grade):**
• Aircraft structural components (wings, fuselage)
• Military equipment
• Rock climbing equipment
• Competitive cycling parts
• Mould tooling for plastics
• Highest strength-to-weight ratio

sections.heading: How to Choose the Right Grade
sections.content:
**Choose 5052 when:**
• Marine/saltwater environment
• Excellent weldability is needed
• Moderate strength is sufficient
• Forming and bending is required

**Choose 6061 when:**
• High strength + good machinability needed
• Structural applications requiring heat treatment
• General-purpose engineering applications
• Good corrosion resistance + strength balance

**Choose 6063 when:**
• Surface finish and appearance matter
• Complex extrusion profiles needed
• Architectural and decorative applications
• Anodizing will be applied

**Choose 7075 when:**
• Maximum strength is required
• Weight savings are critical (aerospace)
• Stress-critical structural components
• Cost and weldability are secondary concerns

Starlight Tubes supplies aluminium pipes, sheets, and bars in all four grades. Contact us for specifications and pricing.

faqSchema.question: Which aluminium alloy is strongest?
faqSchema.answer: Aluminium 7075-T6 is the strongest common aluminium alloy with a tensile strength of 572 MPa and yield strength of 503 MPa. It is used primarily in aerospace and military applications where the highest strength-to-weight ratio is required.

faqSchema.question: Which aluminium grade is best for marine use?
faqSchema.answer: Aluminium 5052 is the best choice for marine and saltwater environments. It has the highest corrosion resistance among common aluminium alloys, excellent weldability, and good formability. It is widely used for boat hulls, marine hardware, and fuel tanks.

title: Stainless Steel Pipe Manufacturing Process Explained
description: Detailed explanation of how stainless steel pipes are manufactured including seamless and welded pipe processes, cold drawing, heat treatment, testing, and quality standards.
keywords: stainless steel pipe manufacturing, seamless pipe manufacturing, welded pipe process, ERW pipe, cold drawn pipe, pipe extrusion, ASTM A312, pipe heat treatment, pipe testing, mandrel mill
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Stainless steel pipes are manufactured through two primary methods: seamless and welded. Each method produces pipes with different characteristics, cost profiles, and suitability for various applications.

Understanding the manufacturing process helps engineers specify the right product and appreciate the testing and quality controls that ensure pipe integrity. This guide walks through both processes step by step.

sections.heading: Seamless Pipe Manufacturing
sections.content:
Seamless pipes have no weld seam, making them stronger and more suitable for high-pressure applications.

**Process Steps:**

**1. Billet Preparation:** Round stainless steel billets (typically 150-300mm diameter) are cut to length and heated to 1200-1300°C in a rotary hearth furnace.

**2. Piercing:** The heated billet is pierced through its center using a piercing mill (Mannesmann process). A piercing plug and two barrel-shaped rolls create a hollow shell.

**3. Elongation:** The hollow shell passes through a mandrel mill (or plug mill) where the wall thickness is reduced and the length is increased.

**4. Sizing:** The pipe passes through a stretch-reducing mill or sizing mill to achieve the final outside diameter.

**5. Heat Treatment:** Solution annealing at 1050-1100°C followed by rapid water quenching to dissolve carbides and restore corrosion resistance.

**6. Straightening:** Rotary straightening to meet straightness tolerances.

**7. Cutting & Finishing:** Cut to ordered length, end-faced, and beveled if required.

sections.heading: Welded Pipe Manufacturing
sections.content:
Welded pipes are more cost-effective and available in larger diameters.

**ERW (Electric Resistance Welded) Process:**
1. Stainless steel coil/strip is uncoiled and leveled
2. Strip edges are trimmed to precise width
3. Strip is progressively formed into a round shape using forming rolls
4. Edges are heated by high-frequency induction and pressed together (no filler metal)
5. Weld flash is removed (ID and OD scarfing)
6. Heat treatment of weld zone
7. Sizing and straightening

**TIG Welded (GTAW) Process:**
Used for high-quality tubing (ASTM A269/A270):
1. Strip is formed into tube shape
2. Edges are TIG welded using argon shielding gas
3. Produces smooth, high-integrity weld with excellent corrosion resistance
4. Preferred for pharmaceutical, sanitary, and instrumentation tubing

**SAW (Submerged Arc Welded) Process:**
For large diameter pipes (typically >16" OD):
1. Plate is formed into pipe by UOE or JCOE process
2. Double submerged arc welded (inside + outside seams)
3. Expanded to final diameter
4. Used for ASTM A358 / ASTM A312 large-bore applications

sections.heading: Heat Treatment
sections.content:
Heat treatment is critical for stainless steel pipes:

**Solution Annealing:**
• Temperature: 1040-1120°C (varies by grade)
• Purpose: Dissolve chromium carbides back into the matrix
• Cooling: Rapid water quench or air cool
• Result: Maximum corrosion resistance and ductility

**Why it matters:** During manufacturing and welding, chromium can combine with carbon to form chromium carbides at grain boundaries (sensitization). This depletes the surrounding matrix of chromium, creating zones susceptible to intergranular corrosion. Solution annealing reverses this.

**Low-Carbon Grades (304L, 316L):** Contain ≤0.03% carbon, significantly reducing sensitization risk. This is why 316L is preferred over 316 for welded applications.

sections.heading: Testing & Quality Control
sections.content:
Every stainless steel pipe undergoes rigorous testing:

**Mandatory Tests:**
• **Hydrostatic Test:** Internal pressure test to verify leak-tightness (per ASTM A530)
• **Eddy Current Test (ET):** Electromagnetic NDE to detect surface and near-surface defects
• **Visual Inspection:** Surface quality, dimensions, markings

**Common Supplementary Tests:**
• **Ultrasonic Test (UT):** Volumetric inspection for internal defects
• **Radiographic Test (RT):** X-ray inspection of weld seams
• **Intergranular Corrosion Test (IGC):** Per ASTM A262 Practice E — verifies resistance to intergranular attack (mandatory for 316L in many specs)
• **Positive Material Identification (PMI):** XRF or OES to verify alloy composition
• **Flattening / Flaring Test:** Ductility verification
• **Hardness Test:** Brinell, Rockwell, or Vickers

**Dimensional Inspection:**
• Outside diameter and ovality
• Wall thickness (minimum wall per specification)
• Length and straightness
• End squareness and bevel angle

Starlight Tubes performs all required tests and provides EN 10204 Type 3.1 Mill Test Certificates with every delivery.

faqSchema.question: What is the difference between seamless and welded stainless steel pipe?
faqSchema.answer: Seamless pipes are made from solid billets by piercing and rolling — they have no weld seam and are stronger, ideal for high-pressure applications. Welded pipes are made from flat strip/coil that is formed and welded — they are more cost-effective, available in larger sizes, and suitable for lower-pressure and structural applications.

faqSchema.question: Why is solution annealing important for stainless steel pipes?
faqSchema.answer: Solution annealing (heating to 1040-1120°C and rapid cooling) dissolves chromium carbides that form during manufacturing. Without annealing, these carbides deplete chromium at grain boundaries, causing intergranular corrosion susceptibility. Solution annealing restores maximum corrosion resistance.

title: How to Choose the Right Pipe Material for Your Project
description: Expert guide on selecting the right pipe material covering stainless steel, carbon steel, nickel alloys, copper, and aluminium based on temperature, pressure, corrosion, cost, and application requirements.
keywords: pipe material selection, how to choose pipe material, stainless steel vs carbon steel pipe, pipe material guide, corrosion resistant pipe, high temperature pipe, pipe specification, piping material
author: Starlight Tubes

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
Selecting the right pipe material is one of the most critical decisions in any piping project. The wrong choice can lead to premature corrosion, mechanical failure, safety hazards, and costly replacements. The right choice balances performance requirements with economic considerations.

This guide walks you through the key factors to consider and provides a decision framework for choosing between stainless steel, carbon steel, nickel alloys, copper, and aluminium pipes.

sections.heading: Key Selection Factors
sections.content:
**1. Operating Temperature:**
• Carbon Steel: -29°C to +425°C (ASTM A106)
• Stainless Steel 316: -196°C to +870°C
• Inconel 625: -196°C to +980°C
• Copper: -198°C to +260°C
• Aluminium: -196°C to +200°C

**2. Pressure Rating:**
• Carbon Steel: Excellent — high wall thickness available, all SCH ratings
• Stainless Steel: Excellent — can match carbon steel pressure ratings
• Nickel Alloys: Excellent — premium pressure service
• Copper: Moderate — typically lower pressure applications
• Aluminium: Moderate — lower strength limits pressure ratings

**3. Corrosion Environment:**
• Atmospheric: Carbon Steel (coated) or Stainless Steel
• Chloride/Seawater: SS 316, Duplex, CuNi 90/10, Inconel 625
• Acids: Hastelloy, Inconel, or appropriate SS grade
• High-purity water: SS 316L, Copper
• Alkaline: Carbon Steel, Nickel alloys

sections.heading: Decision Framework
sections.content:
Follow this decision framework to narrow down your material choice:

**Step 1: Define Service Conditions**
• What is the operating temperature range?
• What is the design pressure?
• What fluid will the pipe carry?
• Is the environment corrosive? (chlorides, acids, H₂S?)

**Step 2: Check Code Requirements**
• What piping code governs the project? (ASME B31.1, B31.3, B31.4, B31.8?)
• Are there material restrictions in the code?
• Is the service classified as Category M (lethal), D (benign), or normal?

**Step 3: Evaluate Corrosion**
• What is the expected corrosion rate?
• Can you add corrosion allowance to carbon steel?
• Is the lifecycle cost of a premium material lower than frequent carbon steel replacement?

**Step 4: Consider Economics**
• Initial material cost vs. lifecycle cost
• Installation cost (some materials require specialized welding)
• Availability and lead time
• Maintenance and inspection costs

sections.heading: Quick Selection Guide by Application
sections.content:
**Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Carbon Steel (API 5L) — cost-effective, well-established codes
**Refinery Process Piping:** Carbon Steel (A106) or SS 316 depending on service
**Chemical Processing:** SS 316L, Hastelloy, or Inconel depending on chemicals
**Marine/Offshore:** Duplex SS, CuNi 90/10, or Inconel 625
**Food & Pharmaceutical:** SS 316L (sanitary grade, electropolished)
**HVAC & Plumbing:** Copper Type L or K
**Structural & Architectural:** Aluminium 6063 or SS 304
**Aerospace:** Inconel 625/718, Aluminium 7075
**Power Generation:** A106 Gr.B (steam), SS 316 (cooling), A671/A672 (main steam)
**Cryogenic Service:** SS 304L, ASTM A333 Gr.6, Aluminium

Starlight Tubes manufactures pipes in all these materials. Our technical team can help you select the right material for your specific application. Contact us for expert material selection assistance.

faqSchema.question: How do I choose between stainless steel and carbon steel pipe?
faqSchema.answer: Choose carbon steel when the environment is non-corrosive, temperatures are moderate (up to 425°C), and cost is a priority. Choose stainless steel when corrosion resistance is needed, the fluid is aggressive, hygiene is important (food/pharma), or the application involves cryogenic temperatures. Carbon steel is 2-3x cheaper but may require coatings and has shorter life in corrosive environments.

faqSchema.question: When should I use nickel alloy pipes instead of stainless steel?
faqSchema.answer: Use nickel alloys (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy) when service conditions exceed stainless steel's capabilities: temperatures above 870°C, exposure to strong acids (HCl, H2SO4), chloride-induced stress corrosion cracking environments, or when the combination of high temperature AND corrosion resistance is needed. Nickel alloys cost 3-10x more than stainless steel but are essential for extreme service.
